ABSTRACT 
 
The Internet of Things (IoT) became the most impactful development 
worldwide. This development influenc

ed many businesses to adapt and shift to 
online business or so -called e -commerce. Amazon as an e -commerce platform 
offered hundreds of millions of products for sale s, as on June 20th, 2017 offered 
372 million products online [1]. With this amount of produc

ts , amazon used the 
Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS) as their warehouse system. In this 
study, a sim ulation of the RMFS was built  used NetLogo  to maximize the pod 
utilization and minimize the energy consumpti

on of the warehouse.  
This study combined 3 SKU to Pod assignments and 4 Replenishment 
policies. SKU to Pod has the role to improve the pod utilizat ion by increase pick 
unit in each visit.  Pod with more SKU types is likely to fulfill more orders.

Replenishment policy has the role to maintain the inventory of the warehouse and 
keep the pod at a high service level. Other than that, replenishment triggers reduced 
visits to the picking station. A pod that doesn’t have sufficient capacity couldn’t be 
assigned with new order although it already has the most order assigned.  
This approach showed improvement by assigned different SKU classes in 
one pod and con sidering SKU inventory also with pod urgency level. Maintained 
the inventory level in the warehouse above 59% of total inventory influenced the 
warehouse performance. There was increasing of 17.83% in pod utilization and 
reducing of 14.75% in energy consumption. 
 
Keywords: Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS) , Pod Replenishment, 
SKUs Classification, Pod Utilization

CHAPTER 1 
INTRODUCTION 
 
 
1.1 Background and Motivation 
In these past few years, the Internet of Things (IoT) became the most 
impactful development worldwide. This development influenced many businesses 
to adapt and shift to online business or so -called e -commerce. As an example, 
Amazon as an e -commerce platform offered hundreds of millions of products for 
sales, as on June 20th, 2017 offered 372 million products online [1]. These types of 
products are stored in a warehouse with proper design that considering warehouse 
management, inventory management, and the internet of things [2].  
Warehouse design has a role to maintain and improve efficiency also reduce 
distribution costs in the warehouse [3]. There are five main groups of warehouse 
systems as “picker-to-parts”, “pick-to-box”, “pick-and-sort”, “parts-to-picker”, and 
“completely automated picking” [4]. In parts-to-picker warehouse integrated with 
the Internet of Things such as data management, sensors, robot, and many other 
things to synchronized all processes in the warehouse. 
Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS) is one breakthrough in the 
implementation of e-commerce warehouses and is categorized as the parts-to-picker 
system. Kiva System as one of the innovative approaches uses hundreds of custom-
built mobile robots (AGVs) that carry small shelving units (Pods) and deliver to the 
assigned stations (Picking or Replenishment Station)  [5]. AGVs are designed to 
lifting the pod, a sensing system to avoid the collision, a navigation system to help 
the movement of AGV , powered by battery, trolley mounted rubber wheels, and a 
control system as the brain of the robot [6]. Picking stations have a role to fulfill the 
picking order and send the products out of the warehouse. Replenishment stations 
have a role to maintain the product's availability in the warehouse.  
The combination of the picking and replenishment station is also supported 
by the pods' design. Pods are assigned by the types of SKUs and amount of SKUs 
quantity stored inside it. This policy is designed to maximize the picking and 
replenishment process. SKUs dimensions, cycles, characteristics, and popularity 
are considered as a factor to assign SKUs to pods [7].  The SKUs need to distribute 

 
2 
 
in all pods with a policy such as : 
1. Random, SKU assigned to the pod with random order. 
2. Product similarity, integrated with order batching or order alienation to consider 
the product similarity by considering 2 - 40 SKUs [8] [9].  
3. Association Rules (AR), considering orders pattern as the base for correlation 
between the SKUs and give a combination of SKUs that can be assigned in the 
pod. Implement this method with a small number of SKUs ( 10 SKUs [10] and 
26 SKUs [11] ) can give feasible result s, however, if a large number of SKUs 
being considered it showed an unfeasible combination.  
4. ABC classification , SK U assigned  base on the popularity proportion of the  
SKUs. Integrating AR and ABC classification is used to design the  SKUs 
assignment to pod by set class-A’s SKUs in all the pod and correlated with other 
classes also SKU with promotional effect [11]. 
Pod utilization is measured base on the SKU’s capability to maintain a high 
picking unit/ visit . Replenishment policy is designed to  avoid stock out of SKUs 
which can delay the orders fulfillment  and reduce pod utilization . In a traditional 
warehouse or “pickers-to-parts” replenishment is always triggered by each SKU ’s 
inventory level. If the inventory level of the SKU reached a certain level it will 
order a certain quantity [12, 13]. However, the RMFS’s replenishment considered 
pod condition which contained many types of SKUs. Pod condition is influenced 
base on the pod’s location, the pod’s free space, the pod’s frequency being picked, 
and the pod ’s inventory level of an SKU is considered as the trigger for the 
replenishment [14, 15] . After the pod was triggered to be replenished, AGV 
delivered the pod to the replenishment station. AGV’s could be assigned only for 
the picking/replenishment process [14] or both processes. This assignm ent also 
influenced the time for the pod to get replenishment. 
In this study, SKUs assignment to pods designed to maximize the pod 
utilization. ABC classification was implemented to assign SKUs in pods based on 
demand popularity. Replenishment policy designed to maintain inventory level of 
the pod. The replenishment is triggered based on the SKUs urgency in the pod. 
Furthermore, by combined these assignments the warehouse could have a reduction 
in energy consumption and increase pod utilization. 

 
3 
 
1.2 Objective 
The objective of this study is to minimize energy consumption and 
maximize pod utilization by  considering the inventory level . The energy 
consumption considered the average number of pods delivered to the picking station 
in an hour. The fewer pods delivered to the picking station means the AGVs didn’t 
need to move around the warehouse too much. Other than that, fewer pods delivered 
need to maximize the pod utilization for maintaining the throughput efficiency of 
the warehouse. 
The pod utilization considered the average number of SKUs being pick in 
each visit. Designed the combination of SKU types to increase more SKUs being 
picked in each visit. More SKUs being picked indicate that the pod quantity got to 
reduce a lot. Low pod capacity influenced the total number of orders being pick in 
each visit. This is maintained by determined the replenishment policy and the 
replenishment level of the pod. Combined the SKU to pod assignment with the 
replenishment policy influenced the objectives. The combined scenario is explained 
in more detail in chapter 3. 
 
1.3 Scope and Limitation 
Few limitations are being used in this study. First, all AGVs are assigned for 
the picking and replenishment process. This means AGVs didn ’t divide to only 
assigned to the picking/replenishment p rocess. Second, all SKUs need to be store 
in the warehouse. Although there are SKUs rarely being order, it's still being stored 
in the warehouse. Third, SKU was differentiated by SKU types and number of units. 
Fourth, the AGV charging process is not yet to be considered, thus focusing on the 
picking and replenishment process. Fifth, the warehouse simulated using NetLogo 
as the main platform of Agent -Based Modelling (ABM). This simulation also 
integrated with python as the optimization platform and excel as the data database 
for storing the result from NetLogo and python. 
  
1.4 Organization of Thesis  
This study consist of five chapters. The organization of it is as follows: 
Chapter 1 presents the problem background and motivation of the study, as well as 

 
4 
 
the objective, and scope also limitation. Chapter 2 explains the literature review of 
related work. Chapter 3 describes the simulation platform, its process flow, and 
parameters. Chapter 4 analyze the proposed improvement, including the result of 
it; and Chapter 5 conclusion and future work for this study.

CHAPTER 2 
LITERATURE REVIEW 
 
 
2.1 Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS) 
RMFS is one of the breakthroughs in warehouse systems to adapt to e -
commerce disruption. This system is supported by AGVs, Pods, Replen ishment 
Station, Picking Station, and Charging Stations. AGVs have the role bring the pod 
to picking or replenishment stations then bring the pod back to the storage area, one 
of the advantages of this system is eliminating the need for humans movement in 
the storage area [14, 16]. Pods have the role of SKU storage which is divided into 
few compartments. AGV can carry a pod with a weight of around 450 – 1300 kg 
depends on the pod size [17]. An example of AGV carries the pod is shown in Figure 
1. 
 
Figure 1. A Pod Carried by AGV 
The process of RMFS started when there is an order enter the system. There 
are 2 types of order, picking order and replenishment order. Picking order assigned 
to the pod which has the required SKU. AGV picked the pod from its location and 
delivered it to the picking station. After finishing the picking process, AGV brought 
back the pod to the empty location in the storage area. Replenishment order  is 
triggered when the pod already reaches a certain inventory level [15]. An illustration 
of the RMFS process is shown in Figure 2. 


 
6 
 
  
Figure 2. Illustration of RMFS Process Flow [14] 
In RMFS implementation there is also a preparation stage to design the 
warehouse. Few things need to be considered like the layout design and SKU 
distribution. In layout design, it determine d based on the total working stations, 
total pods, total  AGVs, and total SKU capacity. In SKUs distribution, it needs to 
consider the total types of SKUs and the pod's capacity. SKUs assignment has a role 
to support the operation stage later on. In the operation stage there are 5 assignments 
referred to as decision problems in RMFS [14] : 
1. Pick Order Assignment (POA), selecting the picking order to be assigned to the 
pod first. 
2. Replenishment Order Assignment (ROA), select ing replenishment station for 
next order. 
3. Pick Pod Selection (PPS), selecting pod to transport to a pick station. 
4. Replenishment Pod Selection (RPS), selecting pod to get replenished. 
5. Pod Storage Assignment (PSA), selecting empty storage location for the pod. 
Other than these assignments RMFS also focuses in set the rout ing of the 
AGVs. Path planning is the most popular method in this by generating a path that 
is followed by the robot [14]. Other than these assignments RMFS also focuses in 
set the routing of the AGVs. Path planning is the most popular method in this by 
generating a path that is followed by the robot  [18, 19, 20] . By designing all of 
these consideration s RMFS can be synchronized to maximize warehouse 
performance. 
 
2.2 SKUs to Pods Assignment 
SKUs to Pods assignment described as SKUs distribution to all pods that 
limited with single type SKU in one pod or multiple types SKU in one pod. This 


 
7 
 
assignment influenced the number of units being picked from the shelf. 3 problems 
were considered like SKU’s types in one pod, SKU quantity in one pod, and SKU 
distribution in other pods [21, 22]. After considering these factors SKUs can be 
assigned as single SKU in one pod and multiple SKUs in one pod (mixed shelves). 
Single SKU in one pod is suitable when there are large quantity orders, however , 
mixed shelves are more suitable in e -commerce which has small quantity orders  
[18].  
Multiple SKUs can be assigned randomly or following a policy to one pod. 
Many policies can be used like SKU similarity [8, 9, 23] , Association Rules, and 
ABC classification [11]. In SKUs similarity, the pod require to maximize all SKUs 
similarity. In Association Rules it has the same objective of SKUs similarity, but it 
will consider the most frequent combination of the SKUs. In ABC classification the 
SKUs are classified by the popularity of the demands and SKU types. The research 
gap summary of previous research is shown in the table below.

2.3 ABC Classification 
ABC Classification is a method to classified a large nu mber of products 
(SKUs) based on specific criteria. The cost criteria based on minimizing total 
inventory cost consider the shortage cost of demand divided by holding cost of the 
order size [24]. ABC classification has the similarity with Pareto’s Law where 20% 
of total effort yields 80% total of outcome [25]. Multiple criteria in the ABC 
Classification design are in table 1. 
 
 
 

 
8 
 
Table 2. Multi Criteria ABC Classification [26, 27]. 
 
Implementation of ABC classification used in inventory with a large number 
of SKUs. Class A which referred to SKUs with high urgency needs to be prioritized 
in the warehouse, class B has less priority, and class C has the lowest priority [28]. 
Inventory management can be design based on the priority of each class to gain a 
high service level of the warehouse. Although class C has the least priority a 
countermeasure needs to be designed to avoid stock out. 
 
2.4 Inventory Management 
Inventory management has an important role in the warehouse management 
system. The main purpose is to maintain the availability of the product in the 
warehouse. A replenishment strategy which not design properly can lead to 
overstock or stock o ut in the warehouse, and this can make a bullwhip effect or 
disruption to the entire supply chain process [28]. There are 3 indicators that are 
considered as the inventory level in the replenishment schedule [29]. 
 
Figure 3. Inventory Status Threshold Setting And Replenishment Time [29]. 
These indicators are design based on the consumption rate of goods, safety 
stock level, minimum stock level, the value of goods, emergency response capacity,

and ease of purchase goods [29]. The figure also showed if the product failed to 
replenish on time, the status was set as Out Of Stock (OOS).  Following the 
indicator, replenishment levels need to be set to determine the minimum level and 
the number to be replenished. Few replenishment policies can be used such as [12, 
13, 30, 31, 32]: 
1. Replenishment based on the system-wide order amount of the local base-stock 
policy. 
2. “Q, S” policy is the total amount of demand since the previous order reaches Q 
units, an order of amount Q to raises the inventory positions of all items to S. 
3. “s, Q” policy is a joint replenishment order of si ze Q is triggered when the 
inventory position of an item falls to its reorder point s.  
4. “R, nQ” policy is a periodic review of inventory position and order minimum 
inventory position to be at or above R. 
5. Stock-Need Rule policy is the lower the stock level of a product compared to 
the needs of the product in a certain wave. 
However, in the RMFS warehouse using a pod that contains single or 
multiple types of SKU , the inventory policy needs to be adjusted. RMFS 
replenishment needs to consider the pod's conditi on which has the most urgent 
condition that needs to be replenished.  The research gap summary of the

CHAPTER 3 
METHODOLOGY 
 
3.1 Simulation Layout 
RMFS warehouse replicates in this simulation layout is divided into 3 places 
like picking station, replenishment station, and storage area. The picking station has 
a function to pick items from the pod. The replenishment station has a function to 
replenish items to the pod. The storage area has the function to store the pods. The 
layout for this simulation is shown in Figure 4. 
 
Figure 4. Layout Simulation 
AGVs are divided into 3 types to assign the pod based on the roles. In station 
areas, there are a worker and queuein g line. Every time AGV comes to the station 
it lined up based on the queuing line. In the storage area, there are f ew elements 
such as : 
1. The intersection is a trigger for AGV to detect the route ahead (intersection).  
2. Selected Pod is a pod that contains some orders and waits to pick.  
3. Empty Storage Location is an available place to store the pod. 
4. The pod is a rack that contains many SKUs.  
5. Pod Batch is the size of the stored pod.  
6. An aisle is a place for AGV to move and design a one-way direction. 
 


 
11 
 
3.2 Process Flow 
RMFS process is divided into few parts based on the relation from each 
resource. The RMFS process flow can be seen in figure 5. 
 
Figure 5. RMFS Process Flow and The Relation Between Each Resource. 
There are 6 resources descri bed such as order, SKU, pod, robot (AGVs), 
picking station, and replenishment station. Between each resource, it started with 
SKU to Pod assignment, order arrival, order to pod assignment, pod sequencing, 
robot to pod assignment, robot routing, robot to st ations (Picking station and 
replenishment station), robot to storage assignment, pod to replenish,  and SKU to 
replenish. Each process is described in more detail in the next sub-chapter. 
 


 
12 
 
3.2.1 SKU to Pod Assignment 
SKU to pod assignment needs to be determined b efore operating the 
warehouse. This study used 3 scenarios, Random used as the baseline, One Pod One 
Class, and Mix Class One Pod assignment. However, the limitation in this study is 
all the SKUs need to be store d in pods. Random assignment randomly assign s the 
SKU to the pod based on the SKU limit in the pod. One class one pod, use ABC 
rule to classify the number of pods based on 60% pod of SKU A, 25% pod of SKU 
B, 15% pod of SKU C. Mix class one pod, ABC rule to classify the number of units 
based on 60% u nits of SKU A, 25% units of SKU B, 15% units of SKU C. The 
detail about SKU distribution is explained in more detail in chapter 4. 
 
3.2.2 Order Arrival  
First, generate the arrival time following a certain distribution. In each 
arrival, it generate d few orders w ith 1 type of SKU. The total amount of orders 
generated are between 1 and 2  [15]. The type of SKU is based on the following 
ABC rule where 10% of the SKU is 60% of the order, 30% of the SKU is 25% of 
the order, and 60% of the SKU is 15% of the order. 
 
3.2.3 Order to Pod Assignment 
After the order is generated it is assigned to the pod immediately. The 
generated order is assigned to the pod which has the earliest due -date order. After 
the pod decided, it checked the capacity of the SKU of the order. SKU with 
sufficient capacity picked as the order to pod result otherwise, it checked another 
pod to assigned. This means one pod could have more than 1 order. 
 
3.2.4 Pod Sequencing 
After the order is assigned to the pod it sort ed the pod based on c ertain 
criteria. The system sorted the pod based on the earliest due date of the order. This 
process has the function to determine the urgency of the order in the pod. 
 
3.2.5 Robot to Pod Assignment 
After the order being assigned to the pod, it is labeled as the  selected pod. 

 
13 
 
Available AGVs are assigned to pick the selected pod with the earliest due date and 
nearest distance. The total pod that is being considered double the number of 
available AGVs. This assignment used manhattan distance to calculate the distance 
[33] and the Hungarian algorithm [34] to pair the AGVs with the pods.  Distance 
calculation consider ed all the possibilities of the selected pod with the starting 
intersection and ending intersection [35]. This distance used as a value to compare 
in the Hungarian algorithm.  
 
3.2.6 Robot Routing 
In this simulation, the routing policy following the previous research using 
Simple Routing with Traffic Policy [35]. The AGVs routing is based on the 
direction in the aisle like shown in Figure 5. Other than that, there is an exception 
where the AGV can move beneath the pod horizontally to the destination in the 
same pod batch. The traffic policy used is dead lock and collision prevention, also 
in each intersection AGV horizontal aisle will be prioritized. 
 
3.2.7 Robot to Stations 
AGV that is already assigned to the selected pod needs to deliver it to the 
station based on the order. In picking or replenishment order the AGV is assigned 
to the station with the least AGV waiting. Assign AGV to the station with more 
AGV increased the queuing time in the station. 
 
3.2.8 Robot to Storage Assignment 
After finish, the picking or replenishment process AGV needs to bring back 
the pod to the storage area. First, list all the possible empty locations in the storage 
area. This list is considered with the AGV which needs to bring back the pod. The 
Hungarian algorithm is used to determine the nearest empty location from the 
station. 
 
3.2.9 Pod to Replenish 
After the pod finishes the picking process it checked the pod condition based 
on the policy. If the pod needs to be replenished, AGV directly deliver s the pod to 

 
14 
 
the replenishment station. Pod replenishment is triggered by a certain policy. 4 
policies used in this study are the Emptiest Pod, Pod Inventory Level, Warehouse 
Inventory-SKU in Pod, and Stockout Probability . The baseline of the pod 
replenishment is Pod Inventory Level with a 60% inventory level. 
 
3.2.10 SKU to Replenish 
In this study, a ded icated policy was implemented for the SKU 
replenishment. The SKU in the pod got replenish with the same types of SKU. Other 
than that, the SKU quantity was replenished back to the maximum capacity.  In 
Warehouse Inventory-SKU in Pod replenished the SKU that need to be replenished 
and in other policies, it is replenished all SKUs in the pod 
 
3.3 Parameter and Assumptions 
In this simulation, there is a lack of information that needs to be assumed. 
These assumptions are described in the parameter. The parameter is shown in the 
table below.

4.1 SKU to Pod Scenarios 
In each SKU to Pod scenario, it has different methods to distribute SKU that 
influence the total capacity of each SKU. There are 3 scenarios Random 
Assignment, One Class One Pod, and Mix Class One Pod. Random assignment set 
as the baseline and compared with other policies. These scenarios will influence the 
distribution of the pod. 
Random assignment assigned without any rule to the pod, other than the 
total SKU type limits and quantity n eeds to be satisfied. One Class One Pod was 
assigned by occupied the pod based on the class with 60% of class A, 25% of class 
B, and 15% of class C. In each pod also has different total SKU type limits based 
on the class where 10 SKUs for class A, 20 SKUs for class B, and 50 for class C. 
Mixed Class One Pod assigned all the classes proportionally in each pod. The SKU 
types limit in one pod is 20 SKUs and the proportion of units are 60% of class A, 
24% of class B, and 16% of class C.

4 replenishment policies were proposed in this study. These policies are 
designed to balancing the invent ory level of the warehouse. The replenishment is 
triggered by the pod urgency after finishing the picking process. These are the  
replenishment policies that used in this study : 
1. Emptiest, firstly it sorted all the pods in the system and labeled the pod which 
has the lowest inventory level. However, if the lowest pod has not been assigned 
to the picking station it will stay idle until an AGV being assigned. 
2. Pod Inventory Level, this policy  is considered  the inventory level of a pod 
without considered the SKU  types. First, calculate the index of the pod

This development influenced many businesses to adapt and shift to online business or so -called e -commerce. As an example, Amazon as an e -commerce platform offered hundreds of millions of products for sales, as on June 20th, 2017 offered 372 million products online [1].

These types of products are stored in a warehouse with proper design that considering warehouse management, inventory management, and the internet of things [2]. Warehouse design has a role to maintain and improve efficiency also reduce distribution costs in the warehouse [3].

There are five main groups of warehouse systems as “picker-to-parts”, “pick-to-box”, “pick-and-sort”, “parts-to-picker”, and “completely automated picking” [4]. In parts-to-picker warehouse integrated with the Internet of Things such as data management, sensors, robot, and many other things to synchronized all processes in the warehouse.

Robotic Mobile Fulfillment System (RMFS) is one breakthrough in the implementation of e-commerce warehouses and is categorized as the parts-to-picker system. Kiva System as one of the innovative approaches uses hundreds of custom- built mobile robots (AGVs) that carry small shelving units (Pods) and deliver to the assigned stations (Picking or Replenishment Station) [5]. AGVs are designed to lifting the pod, a sensing system to avoid the collision, a navigation system to help the movement of AGV , powered by battery, trolley mounted rubber wheels, and a control system as the brain of the robot [6].

Picking stations have a role to fulfill the picking order and send the products out of the warehouse. Replenishment stations have a role to maintain the product's availability in the warehouse. The combination of the picking and replenishment station is also supported by the pods' design. Pods are assigned by the types of SKUs and amount of SKUs quantity stored inside it. This policy is designed to maximize the picking and replenishment process.

SKUs dimensions, cycles, characteristics, and popularity are considered as a factor to assign SKUs to pods [7]. The SKUs need to distribute 2 in all pods with a policy such as : 1. Random, SKU assigned to the pod with random order. 2. Product similarity, integrated with order batching or order alienation to consider the product similarity by considering 2 - 40 SKUs [8] [9]. 3. Association Rules (AR), considering orders pattern as the base for correlation between the SKUs and give a combination of SKUs that can be assigned in the pod. Implement this method with a small number of SKUs ( 10 SKUs [10] and 26 SKUs [11] ) can give feasible result s, however, if a large number of SKUs being considered it showed an unfeasible combination. 4.

ABC classification , SK U assigned base on the popularity proportion of the SKUs. Integrating AR and ABC classification is used to design the SKUs assignment to pod by set class-A’s SKUs in all the pod and correlated with other classes also SKU with promotional effect [11].

Pod utilization is measured base on the SKU’s capability to maintain a high picking unit/ visit . Replenishment policy is designed to avoid stock out of SKUs which can delay the orders fulfillment and reduce pod utilization .

In a traditional warehouse or “pickers-to-parts” replenishment is always triggered by each SKU ’s inventory level. If the inventory level of the SKU reached a certain level it will order a certain quantity [12, 13].

However, the RMFS’s replenishment considered pod condition which contained many types of SKUs. Pod condition is influenced base on the pod’s location, the pod’s free space, the pod’s frequency being picked, and the pod ’s inventory level of an SKU is considered as the trigger for the replenishment [14, 15] . After the pod was triggered to be replenished, AGV delivered the pod to the replenishment station.
AGV’s could be assigned only for the picking/replenishment process [14] or both processes. This assignm ent also influenced the time for the pod to get replenishment.
In this study, SKUs assignment to pods designed to maximize the pod utilization. ABC classification was implemented to assign SKUs in pods based on demand popularity.
Replenishment policy designed to maintain inventory level of the pod. The replenishment is triggered based on the SKUs urgency in the pod.

Furthermore, by combined these assignments the warehouse could have a reduction in energy consumption and increase pod utilization. 3 1.2 Objective The objective of this study is to minimize energy consumption and maximize pod utilization by considering the inventory level . The energy consumption considered the average number of pods delivered to the picking station in an hour.

The fewer pods delivered to the picking station means the AGVs didn’t need to move around the warehouse too much. Other than that, fewer pods delivered need to maximize the pod utilization for maintaining the throughput efficiency of the warehouse.

The pod utilization considered the average number of SKUs being pick in each visit. Designed the combination of SKU types to increase more SKUs being picked in each visit. More SKUs being picked indicate that the pod quantity got to reduce a lot. Low pod capacity influenced the total number of orders being pick in each visit.

This is maintained by determined the replenishment policy and the replenishment level of the pod. Combined the SKU to pod assignment with the replenishment policy influenced the objectives. The combined scenario is explained in more detail in chapter 3. 1.3 Scope and Limitation Few limitations are being used in this study.
First, all AGVs are assigned for the picking and replenishment process. This means AGVs didn ’t divide to only assigned to the picking/replenishment p rocess.

Second, all SKUs need to be store in the warehouse. Although there are SKUs rarely being order, it's still being stored in the warehouse. Third, SKU was differentiated by SKU types and number of units. Fourth, the AGV charging process is not yet to be considered, thus focusing on the picking and replenishment process.

Fifth, the warehouse simulated using NetLogo as the main platform of Agent -Based Modelling (ABM). This simulation also integrated with python as the optimization platform and excel as the data database for storing the result from NetLogo and python.

The organization of it is as follows: Chapter 1 presents the problem background and motivation of the study, as well as 4 the objective, and scope also limitation. Chapter 2 explains the literature review of related work. Chapter 3 describes the simulation platform, its process flow, and parameters. Chapter 4 analyze the proposed improvement, including the result of it; and Chapter 5 conclusion and future work for this study. 5 CHAPTER 2 LITERATURE REVIEW 2.1 Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS) RMFS is one of the breakthroughs in warehouse systems to adapt to e - commerce disruption.

This system is supported by AGVs, Pods, Replen ishment Station, Picking Station, and Charging Stations. AGVs have the role bring the pod to picking or replenishment stations then bring the pod back to the storage area, one of the advantages of this system is eliminating the need for humans movement in the storage area [14, 16]. Pods have the role of SKU storage which is divided into few compartments. AGV can carry a pod with a weight of around 450 – 1300 kg depends on the pod size [17]. An example of AGV carries the pod is shown in Figure 1. Figure 1. A Pod Carried by AGV The process of RMFS started when there is an order enter the system. There are 2 types of order, picking order and replenishment order.

Picking order assigned to the pod which has the required SKU. AGV picked the pod from its location and delivered it to the picking station. After finishing the picking process, AGV brought back the pod to the empty location in the storage area. Replenishment order is triggered when the pod already reaches a certain inventory level [15]. An illustration of the RMFS process is shown in Figure 2. 6 Figure 2. Illustration of RMFS Process Flow [14] In RMFS implementation there is also a preparation stage to design the warehouse.
Few things need to be considered like the layout design and SKU distribution. In layout design, it determine d based on the total working stations, total pods, total AGVs, and total SKU capacity.

In SKUs distribution, it needs to consider the total types of SKUs and the pod's capacity. SKUs assignment has a role to support the operation stage later on. In the operation stage there are 5 assignments referred to as decision problems in RMFS [14] : 1. Pick Order Assignment (POA), selecting the picking order to be assigned to the pod first. 2. Replenishment Order Assignment (ROA), select ing replenishment station for next order. 3. Pick Pod Selection (PPS), selecting pod to transport to a pick station. 4. Replenishment Pod Selection (RPS), selecting pod to get replenished. 5. Pod Storage Assignment (PSA), selecting empty storage location for the pod. Other than these assignments RMFS also focuses in set the rout ing of the AGVs. Path planning is the most popular method in this by generating a path that is followed by the robot [14]. Other than these assignments RMFS also focuses in set the routing of the AGVs. Path planning is the most popular method in this by generating a path that is followed by the robot [18, 19, 20] . By designing all of these consideration s RMFS can be synchronized to maximize warehouse performance. 2.2 SKUs to Pods Assignment SKUs to Pods assignment described as SKUs distribution to all pods that limited with single type SKU in one pod or multiple types SKU in one pod. This 7 assignment influenced the number of units being picked from the shelf.

3 problems were considered like SKU’s types in one pod, SKU quantity in one pod, and SKU distribution in other pods [21, 22]. After considering these factors SKUs can be assigned as single SKU in one pod and multiple SKUs in one pod (mixed shelves). Single SKU in one pod is suitable when there are large quantity orders, however , mixed shelves are more suitable in e -commerce which has small quantity orders [18]. Multiple SKUs can be assigned randomly or following a policy to one pod. Many policies can be used like SKU similarity [8, 9, 23] , Association Rules, and ABC classification [11].

In SKUs similarity, the pod require to maximize all SKUs similarity. In Association Rules it has the same objective of SKUs similarity, but it will consider the most frequent combination of the SKUs. In ABC classification the SKUs are classified by the popularity of the demands and SKU types. The research gap summary of previous research is shown in the table below. Table 1.

A replenishment strategy which not design properly can lead to overstock or stock o ut in the warehouse, and this can make a bullwhip effect or disruption to the entire supply chain process [28]. Stock-Need Rule policy is the lower the stock level of a product compared to the needs of the product in a certain wave.

11 3.2 Process Flow RMFS process is divided into few parts based on the relation from each resource. The RMFS process flow can be seen in figure 5. Figure 5. RMFS Process Flow and The Relation Between Each Resource.

There are 6 resources descri bed such as order, SKU, pod, robot (AGVs), picking station, and replenishment station. Between each resource, it started with SKU to Pod assignment, order arrival, order to pod assignment, pod sequencing, robot to pod assignment, robot routing, robot to st ations (Picking station and replenishment station), robot to storage assignment, pod to replenish, and SKU to replenish. Each process is described in more detail in the next sub-chapter.

12 3.2.1 SKU to Pod Assignment SKU to pod assignment needs to be determined b efore operating the warehouse. This study used 3 scenarios, Random used as the baseline, One Pod One Class, and Mix Class One Pod assignment.

However, the limitation in this study is all the SKUs need to be store d in pods. Random assignment randomly assign s the SKU to the pod based on the SKU limit in the pod. One class one pod, use ABC rule to classify the number of pods based on 60% pod of SKU A, 25% pod of SKU B, 15% pod of SKU C. Mix class one pod, ABC rule to classify the number of units based on 60% u nits of SKU A, 25% units of SKU B, 15% units of SKU C. The detail about SKU distribution is explained in more detail in chapter 4.

3.2.2 Order Arrival First, generate the arrival time following a certain distribution. In each arrival, it generate d few orders w ith 1 type of SKU. The total amount of orders generated are between 1 and 2 [15]. The type of SKU is based on the following ABC rule where 10% of the SKU is 60% of the order, 30% of the SKU is 25% of the order, and 60% of the SKU is 15% of the order.
3.2.3 Order to Pod Assignment After the order is generated it is assigned to the pod immediately. The generated order is assigned to the pod which has the earliest due -date order.

After the pod decided, it checked the capacity of the SKU of the order. SKU with sufficient capacity picked as the order to pod result otherwise, it checked another pod to assigned. This means one pod could have more than 1 order. 3.2.4 Pod Sequencing After the order is assigned to the pod it sort ed the pod based on c ertain criteria. The system sorted the pod based on the earliest due date of the order. This process has the function to determine the urgency of the order in the pod. 3.2.5 Robot to Pod Assignment After the order being assigned to the pod, it is labeled as the selected pod.
13 Available AGVs are assigned to pick the selected pod with the earliest due date and nearest distance. The total pod that is being considered double the number of available AGVs.

This assignment used manhattan distance to calculate the distance [33] and the Hungarian algorithm [34] to pair the AGVs with the pods. Distance calculation consider ed all the possibilities of the selected pod with the starting intersection and ending intersection [35]. This distance used as a value to compare in the Hungarian algorithm. 3.2.6 Robot Routing In this simulation, the routing policy following the previous research using Simple Routing with Traffic Policy [35].

The AGVs routing is based on the direction in the aisle like shown in Figure 5. Other than that, there is an exception where the AGV can move beneath the pod horizontally to the destination in the same pod batch. The traffic policy used is dead lock and collision prevention, also in each intersection AGV horizontal aisle will be prioritized.

3.2.7 Robot to Stations AGV that is already assigned to the selected pod needs to deliver it to the station based on the order. In picking or replenishment order the AGV is assigned to the station with the least AGV waiting. Assign AGV to the station with more AGV increased the queuing time in the station. 3.2.8 Robot to Storage Assignment After finish, the picking or replenishment process AGV needs to bring back the pod to the storage area. First, list all the possible empty locations in the storage area. This list is considered with the AGV which needs to bring back the pod. The Hungarian algorithm is used to determine the nearest empty location from the station.

3.2.9 Pod to Replenish After the pod finishes the picking process it checked the pod condition based on the policy. If the pod needs to be replenished, AGV directly deliver s the pod to 14 the replenishment station. Pod replenishment is triggered by a certain policy. 4 policies used in this study are the Emptiest Pod, Pod Inventory Level, Warehouse Inventory-SKU in Pod, and Stockout Probability . The baseline of the pod replenishment is Pod Inventory Level with a 60% inventory level.

3.2.10 SKU to Replenish In this study, a ded icated policy was implemented for the SKU replenishment. The SKU in the pod got replenish with the same types of SKU. Other than that, the SKU quantity was replenished back to the maximum capacity. In Warehouse Inventory-SKU in Pod replenished the SKU that need to be replenished and in other policies, it is replenished all SKUs in the pod 3.3 Parameter and Assumptions In this simulation, there is a lack of information that needs to be assumed.

18 CHAPTER 4 RESULT AND DISCUSSION 4.1 SKU to Pod Scenarios In each SKU to Pod scenario, it has different methods to distribute SKU that influence the total capacity of each SKU. There are 3 scenarios Random Assignment, One Class One Pod, and Mix Class One Pod. Random assignment set as the baseline and compared with other policies.

These scenarios will influence the distribution of the pod. Random assignment assigned without any rule to the pod, other than the total SKU type limits and quantity n eeds to be satisfied. One Class One Pod was assigned by occupied the pod based on the class with 60% of class A, 25% of class B, and 15% of class C. In each pod also has different total SKU type limits based on the class where 10 SKUs for class A, 20 SKUs for class B, and 50 for class C. Mixed Class One Pod assigned all the classes proportionally in each pod. The SKU types limit in one pod is 20 SKUs and the proportion of units are 60% of class A, 24% of class B, and 16% of class C. 4.2 Replenishment Policy In this chapter replenishment method is described in the description and equation.

This system needs to have a proper design to adapt to the e-commerce order.

However, this system ne eds to consider many factors to minimize energy consumption and maximize pod utilization. The implementation of the scenarios of SKU to Pod assignment and replenishment policy influence d the performance of the system. SKU to Pod has the role to improve the pod utilization by increase pick unit in each visit. Pod with more SKU types is likely to fulfill more orders. Replenishment policy has the role to maintain the inventory of the warehouse and keep the pod at a high service level. Other than that, replenishment triggers reduced visits to the picking station.

A pod that doesn’t have sufficient capacity couldn’t be assigned with new order although it already has the most order assigned. There are 3 scenarios in the SKU to Pod assignments such as Random and ABC Classification which is divided as One Class One Pod and Mixed Class One Pod. There are 4 scenarios in the replenishment policies as the Emptiest, Pod Inventory Level, Stockout Probability, and Warehouse Inventory – SKU in Pod. The baseline of this study is Random-Pod Inventory Level with a 60% inventory level.

This baseline is compared with other combined scenarios to find the best result. The result of each scenario is verified using ANOV A and Tukey Pairwise Comparison. The best result is Mixed Class One Pod combined with the Warehouse Inventory SKU in Pod. There is a slight increase of 0.56% of throughput efficiency other than that, the energy consumption reduced by 14.75% pod visit and increased the pod utilization by 17.83% compared to baseline. These results can be achieved by maintaining an average of 59.26% inventory of the warehouse. 5.2 Future Research There are many possibilities in this RMFS research that still can be developed.

The replenishment policy can implement flexible replenishment of SKU 44 to the pod. The pod can get replenished by a new type of SKU. Replacing less popular SKUs with more popular SKUs can increase pod utilization.
In SKU to pod assignment can research by considering the product volume and weight. These two considerations can decide the number of the compartment in a pod.
